text.title: Hallucination Detection — Online Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Hallucination Detection — Online application must enforce. HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Apply Hallucination Detection — Online only to a single instance of the recurring decision. Never batch multiple unrelated decisions through one pass.

rationale.source: Anti-conflation pattern / Datadog LLM Observability hallucination detection / Braintrust 2026

rule.rationale: Batching turns a 1-decision artefact into a debate club. Each instance gets its own record so the rationale is traceable.

rule.statement: Every input field has a declared type and source. Free-form prose without a source citation is rejected.
rule.rationale: Untyped inputs let LLMs hallucinate plausible-but-wrong values. Typed + sourced inputs make the output falsifiable.

rule.statement: The output names a single accountable owner (handle, email, or org role with rotation). No 'team' or 'we' as owner.
rule.rationale: Plural ownership = no ownership. Future readers need a named human to chase when the artefact is stale.

rule.statement: Output carries semver-style version and last_reviewed date. Stale records (over 90 days, no review) must be flagged on read.
rule.rationale: Methodology outputs decay; treating them as immutable is a defect. Versioning + review-by-date forces refresh cadence.

rule.statement: Every claim in the output cites the input artefact(s) that justified it. Untraceable claims are rejected.

rationale.source: Audit-trail discipline / Datadog LLM Observability hallucination detection / Braintrust 2026

rule.rationale: Without traceability, the artefact becomes opinion. Citations make the output reviewable by a future operator with no context.
